decisions.rationale: The block connects maximum-likelihood score solving to root finding but does not identify the Statistical Model endpoint; that connection needs an unstated intermediate.
The blocks directly combine the step size and gradient in the stated gradient-descent displacement and update.
The block explicitly assigns complementary terms of the same second-order approximation to the gradient and Hessian.
Newton root finding is connected to maximum-likelihood score solving, while the Statistical Model endpoint is not identified.
Ordinary Least Squares is absent from the selected block; linking it through Gaussian MLE would require omitted context.
The block directly states that gradient descent is applied using the gradient of the least-squares objective.
The block names a Gaussian linear-regression model, but generic Statistical Model to Linear Regression is only instance membership under this Evidence protocol.
The block links Taylor approximation to Forward Euler but never identifies an Initial Value Problem; the proposed edge requires that intermediate.
The block directly presents finding the maximum-likelihood score-equation candidate as a root-finding problem.
The Evidence says gradient descent applies to Ordinary Least Squares, not directly to the Linear Regression endpoint.
The same block identifies the root-finding problem and explicitly states that Newton's root-finding method can solve it.
The selected block does not identify the referenced formula as the Mean Squared Error Formula, so that endpoint is unresolved.
The block states that solving the score equation is a root-finding problem, directly connecting the score function's zero condition to root finding.
The block directly defines the score used by Maximum Likelihood Estimation as a gradient of its log-likelihood objective.
The block connects a specific Gaussian linear-regression model to least squares; treating this as a generic Statistical Model edge is instance membership.
The text introduces the update from the gradient of the objective and the formula directly contains that objective gradient.
The blocks define the Score Function as a gradient but do not present the Score Equation zero condition named by the endpoint.
The block connects Newton optimisation to the Hessian, but the Objective Function endpoint is not independently identified.
The block explicitly places log-likelihood maximisation inside a Gaussian linear-regression model.
The selected sentence directly defines the Score Function as the gradient of the log-likelihood.
The block directly states the conditional equivalence between maximising log-likelihood and minimising the least-squares objective.
The blocks introduce the score within Maximum Likelihood Estimation and provide its defining formula.
The selected block uses an unresolved 'This formula' reference and never identifies it as the Mean Squared Error Formula.
The block explicitly states that Maximum Likelihood Estimation treats the log-likelihood as an objective function.
The block directly states that the Newton optimisation method requires the Hessian matrix.
The block names a least-squares objective but does not establish a graph relation to the generic Objective Function endpoint beyond instance membership.
The block directly states that Ordinary Least Squares and Maximum Likelihood Estimation select the same coefficients under the stated assumptions.
The blocks directly express the score as the gradient of the log-likelihood.
Objective gradient and step size are separate inputs to Gradient Descent; the selected sentence does not establish a direct predicate between those endpoints.
Linear Regression is absent; reaching it through Maximum Likelihood Estimation requires unstated context.
The block names Ordinary Least Squares and an objective but does not self-containedly identify that objective as the Least Squares Objective endpoint.
The blocks define a root-finding problem, introduce Newton's method for it, and provide the corresponding Newton root update.
The Score Equation endpoint is not present in the selected block; connecting it through log-likelihood is an omitted intermediate step.
The block directly relates log-likelihood maximisation to the least-squares optimisation performed by Ordinary Least Squares.
The block explicitly states that the first-order Taylor approximation predicts change in the objective.
The blocks give the Mean Squared Error Formula and explicitly identify it with the least-squares objective used by Ordinary Least Squares.
The blocks directly give Maximum Likelihood Estimation as an interpretation of fitting Gaussian linear regression.
The selected blocks define the Score Function, not the Score Equation zero condition claimed by the endpoint and rationale.
The blocks state that Linear Regression is fitted by the least-squares objective and supply its Mean Squared Error Formula.
The block directly states that Ridge Regression changes the optimisation objective by adding a penalty.
The blocks state that first-order Taylor approximation yields Forward Euler and immediately provide the Forward Euler update.
The block explicitly states that solving the Score Equation is a root-finding problem.
The selected block refers to 'This formula' without including or naming the Mean Squared Error Formula, leaving that endpoint unresolved.
The block links Newton root finding to the score equation but never identifies the Log-Likelihood endpoint.
The block connects log-likelihood to Objective Function but does not identify a Statistical Model endpoint.
The block directly associates a Gaussian linear-regression model with minimising the least-squares objective.
The block explicitly states that the Newton root update formalizes Newton's root-finding method.
The block directly states that Gradient Descent requires the gradient of the objective function.
The selected block presents a score-equation root-finding problem but does not identify Log-Likelihood.
The sentence identifies log-likelihood as the objective and directly defines its gradient as the Score Function.
The block directly applies Newton's root-finding method to the zero condition of the Score Function.
The root-finding statement concerns a score equation; the Objective Function endpoint is only reachable through omitted log-likelihood context.
The block explicitly states that Gradient Descent requires the gradient of the objective function.
The block directly associates Ordinary Least Squares with minimising the least-squares objective under the stated model.
The block directly states the conditional equivalence between Maximum Likelihood Estimation and minimising the least-squares objective.
The block explains a descending displacement but does not identify or display the Gradient Descent Update endpoint.
The blocks directly state and formalize the use of the gradient in the first-order Taylor approximation.
The block directly places Ordinary Least Squares as the fitting method for the stated Gaussian linear-regression model.
The blocks provide the Newton optimisation update but do not self-containedly identify the Objective Function endpoint.
The selected block identifies a score-equation root-finding problem but never states that the score is a gradient.
The block explicitly states that Gradient Descent requires the gradient of the objective function.
